1.question: Can you tell if a dot product of 2 vectors are on the same line?

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: Yes, if the dot product equals plus or minus the product of their magnitudes
multipleChoiceAnswers.2: No, the dot product never reveals this
multipleChoiceAnswers.3: Yes, but only if the dot product is zero
multipleChoiceAnswers.4: Only if both vectors have length 1

1.correctAnswer: Yes, if the dot product equals plus or minus the product of their magnitudes

2.question: Is there a way to tell the angle between 2 vectors based on the dot product?

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: Yes, using cos(θ)=(a·b)/(|a||b|)
multipleChoiceAnswers.2: No, dot product gives no angle information
multipleChoiceAnswers.4: Yes, the angle equals the dot product

3.question: Is it possible to find the dot product of the vectors [1,2,3] and [1,2,3,4]

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: No, vectors must have the same number of dimensions
multipleChoiceAnswers.3: Yes, the extra term is dropped
multipleChoiceAnswers.4: No, dot products only work for 2D vectors

3.correctAnswer: No, vectors must have the same number of dimensions

4.question: What is the dot product of the vectors [0,1], and [1,0]?

5.question: What is the dot product of the vectors [-1,1] and [1,-1]?

6.question: What does the result of the vectors in the previous 2 questions mean?

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: The first pair is perpendicular (dot product 0), and the second pair has an angle greater than 90° (negative dot product)
multipleChoiceAnswers.2: Both pairs of vectors are identical
multipleChoiceAnswers.3: Both pairs of vectors are parallel
multipleChoiceAnswers.4: The dot products show both pairs are unit vectors

6.correctAnswer: The first pair is perpendicular (dot product 0), and the second pair has an angle greater than 90° (negative dot product)

7.question: What is the dot product of the vectors [1,2,3] and [-1,-2,3]?

8.question: What is the dot product of the 0 vector, a vector v?

9.question: What is the dot product of the 1 vector, and vector v

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: The sum of the components of v
multipleChoiceAnswers.4: The product of the components of v

9.correctAnswer: The sum of the components of v

10.question: What is the dot product of the vector v and vector v

1.question: Is it possible to take the cross product of [1]x[1]

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: No, cross product requires 3D vectors (or 7D)

2.question: Is it possible to take the cross product of [1,2]x[1,2]

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: No, cross product requires 3D vectors (or 7D)

3.question: Is it possible to take the cross product of [1,2,3] x [1,2,3]

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: Yes, and the result is [0,0,0] since the vectors are parallel
multipleChoiceAnswers.2: No, cross product requires two different vectors
multipleChoiceAnswers.4: No, cross product only works with 2D vectors

3.correctAnswer: Yes, and the result is [0,0,0] since the vectors are parallel

1.question: Is multiplying $\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{bmatrix}$ a 2x2 matrix by $\begin{bmatrix}1\end{bmatrix}$ a 1x1 matrix possible?

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: No, the inner dimensions (2 and 1) don't match

1.correctAnswer: No, the inner dimensions (2 and 1) don't match

2.question: Is multiplying $\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{bmatrix}$ a 2x2 matrix by $\begin{bmatrix}1&0\end{bmatrix}$ a 1x2 matrix possible?

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: No, the inner dimensions (2 and 1) don't match

2.correctAnswer: No, the inner dimensions (2 and 1) don't match

3.question: Is multiplying $\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{bmatrix}$ a 2x2 matrix by $\begin{bmatrix}1\\0\end{bmatrix}$ a 2x1 matrix possible?

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: Yes, the result is a 2x1 matrix
multipleChoiceAnswers.3: Yes, the result is a 2x2 matrix
multipleChoiceAnswers.4: Yes, the result is a 1x2 matrix

3.correctAnswer: Yes, the result is a 2x1 matrix

4.question: What is the result of multiplying the matrices $\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{bmatrix}$ by $\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\1&1\end{bmatrix}$?

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: $\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\1&1\end{bmatrix}$

5.question: What is the result of multiplying the matrices $\begin{bmatrix}1&2\\1&3\end{bmatrix}$ by $\begin{bmatrix}1&1\\1&1\end{bmatrix}$?

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: $\begin{bmatrix}3&3\\4&4\end{bmatrix}$

6.question: Is this the same result as multiplying $\begin{bmatrix}1&1\\1&1\end{bmatrix}$ by $\begin{bmatrix}1&2\\1&3\end{bmatrix}$?

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: No, matrix multiplication is not commutative
multipleChoiceAnswers.2: Yes, matrix multiplication is always commutative
multipleChoiceAnswers.3: Yes, because both matrices contain the same numbers
multipleChoiceAnswers.4: No, because the matrices are different sizes

6.correctAnswer: No, matrix multiplication is not commutative

7.question: When multiplying the matrix $\begin{bmatrix}1&1\\1&-1\end{bmatrix}$ by its inverse $\begin{bmatrix}0.5&0.5\\0.5&-0.5\end{bmatrix}$ what is the result?

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: $\begin{bmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{bmatrix}$

8.question: What is the result of the matrix multiplication $\begin{bmatrix}1&1\\2&2\end{bmatrix}$ squared?

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: $\begin{bmatrix}3&3\\6&6\end{bmatrix}$

9.question: Is it possible to multiply the matrix $\begin{bmatrix}1&2&3\\1&2&3\end{bmatrix}$ by itself?

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: No, the inner dimensions (3 and 2) don't match

9.correctAnswer: No, the inner dimensions (3 and 2) don't match

10.question: What could you change about the previous matrix (if anything) to make it multiplyable by itself?

multipleChoiceAnswers.1: Multiply it by its transpose instead (A x $A^T$ or $A^T$ x A)

10.correctAnswer: Multiply it by its transpose instead (A x $A^T$ or $A^T$ x A)
